title: Named exports
instruction:
Named exports let you export multiple things from one file. The importer picks what they need.

Since we're in a single-file environment, we'll simulate modules by demonstrating the patterns inline. Run this to see the structure.
successMessage: Named exports export multiple things by name. Importers choose exactly what they need: import { add, multiply } — clean and explicit.

title: ES Modules syntax
successMessage: Intl.NumberFormat for currency — built into JavaScript! formatDate and formatCurrency are named exports. The default export would be the createUtils factory.

title: CommonJS — require/module.exports
successMessage: CommonJS is synchronous and uses require(). ES Modules are asynchronous and use import. New projects: use ES Modules (.mjs or "type": "module" in package.json).

title: Re-exporting — barrel files
successMessage: Barrel files centralise imports. Instead of import { x } from './utils/string' and import { y } from './utils/number', one import { x, y } from './utils' does it. Very common in React projects.

title: Dynamic imports
instruction:
Regular `import` is static — it runs at module load time. **Dynamic imports** load code on demand:

```js
const module = await import('./heavy-module.js');
```

Useful for:
- Code splitting (load only what's needed)
- Conditional loading (load analytics only in production)
- Lazy loading (load a feature only when the user opens it)

Simulate lazy loading.
successMessage: Dynamic imports are how Next.js, Vite, and webpack do code splitting. Your users download code only when they need it — not everything upfront.

title: import.meta
instruction:
`import.meta` provides metadata about the current module:

- `import.meta.url` — the URL of the current module file
- `import.meta.env` — environment variables (in Vite/build tools)

In Node.js, this replaces the old `__filename` and `__dirname` for ES Modules.
successMessage: import.meta.url replaces __filename in ES Modules. import.meta.env is how Vite exposes .env variables to the browser. Modern tooling uses import.meta extensively.

title: Build a plugin system
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a plugin system using module patterns:

```js
const app = createApp();
app.use(loggerPlugin);
app.use(authPlugin);
app.run(request);
```

Requirements:
- `createApp()` returns an app with `use(plugin)` and `run(context)` methods
- Each plugin is a function that receives and transforms context
- `run()` passes context through each plugin in order
- Built-in plugin: `loggerPlugin` that logs the context
- Built-in plugin: `authPlugin` that adds `user: 'guest'` if no user
hint: function createApp() { const plugins = []; return { use(p) { plugins.push(p); return this; }, run(ctx) { return plugins.reduce((c, p) => p(c), ctx); } }; }
successMessage: A plugin/middleware system — this is exactly how Express.js middleware works, how Koa works, how Vite plugins work. Modules + patterns = architecture. Chapter 16 done!
